title: India's ₹52K Crore Arms Push, F-35 Readiness at 25%, NATO Summit Kicks Off in Ankara
subhead: India approves a massive counter-drone and air defence package, a GAO report reveals only a quarter of F-35s are fully mission capable, and NATO leaders gather in Ankara amid transatlantic burden-sharing tensions and a potential F-35 sale to Turkey.

sections.heading: India Clears ₹52,000 Crore in Defence Procurements
sections.body:
India's Defence Acquisition Council, chaired by Defence Minister Rajnath Singh, approved capital acquisition proposals worth ₹52,000 crore (approximately $5.5 billion) on July 4, with a pronounced emphasis on air defence and counter-drone systems shaped by last year's conflict with Pakistan.

The Indian Army will receive the Akash Tarang anti-drone electronic warfare system, man-portable anti-tank guided missiles, medium-range surface-to-air missiles (MRSAM), very short-range air defence systems (V-SHORADS), active protection systems for tanks, and jet-based kamikaze drone systems. The Navy's approvals cover multi-influence ground mines, shipborne unmanned aerial systems, and a land-based testing facility for electric propulsion systems for future warships. The Indian Air Force will acquire fixed-wing high-altitude pseudo satellite (FW-HAPS) platforms operating in the stratosphere at roughly 20 km altitude for persistent ISR and secure communications, according to Rediff and RNA Media.

The package signals a shift from expeditionary procurement toward indigenous capability development, with the Defence Ministry noting the approvals reflect lessons from the 2025 India-Pakistan confrontation that saw widespread drone use. India's Weekly Blitz called it 'a new era of military strategy' rooted in layered air defence.
sections.whyItMatters: India is institutionalising counter-drone and layered air defence as core priorities — not just buying platforms but building an indigenous industrial base. The FW-HAPS investment puts India among a small group of nations pursuing stratospheric persistent surveillance. The scale of the package, approved in a single DAC session, also signals bureaucratic momentum that could accelerate future procurements.

sections.heading: Only 1 in 4 F-35s Fully Mission Capable as Readiness Declines
sections.body:
A US Government Accountability Office report released in June found that the F-35 fleet's full mission capable rate fell to 25% through fiscal 2025, down from 38% in fiscal 2021. The broader mission capable rate — measuring ability to perform at least one assigned mission — dropped from 67% to 44% over the same period, Military Times reported.

Air Force officials attributed part of the fiscal 2025 decline to new jets that could not perform missions due to software delays, along with scarce parts and corrosion problems. A 2025 Lockheed Martin study identified 48 parts where the supplier base cannot produce enough, including canopies — a top driver of grounded jets.

The F-35 Joint Program Office's response, the Global Support Solution Reset launched in June 2025, targets 80% mission capable and 65% full mission capable rates by 2030. But JPO officials told GAO that readiness will likely worsen before it improves, and GAO estimates the Reset will cost $13.7 billion more than previously planned through fiscal 2031, of which only $2.2 billion is for the Reset itself — the remaining $11.5 billion covers the gap between what services budgeted and what sustainment actually costs.

GAO also found that from 2020 through 2023, the JPO paid Lockheed more than $114 million of roughly $269 million in available incentive fees even as readiness metrics stagnated or worsened. In 19 of 39 performance periods, the JPO and Lockheed adjusted recorded full mission capable rates upward, citing factors outside the company's control. GAO has now made 46 recommendations on F-35 sustainment since 2014; as of March 2026, the Pentagon had implemented 14.
sections.whyItMatters: The F-35 is the Pentagon's most costly weapon system and the backbone of allied air power across NATO and the Indo-Pacific. A 25% full mission capable rate means that at any given time, three-quarters of the fleet cannot perform all assigned missions. With multiple allies — including potentially Turkey — seeking F-35s, the sustainment crisis directly affects alliance readiness and credibility.

sections.heading: NATO Summit in Ankara: Defense Industry Forum, F-35s for Turkey, and Burden-Sharing Showdown
sections.body:
The NATO summit convenes in Ankara on July 7-8 with a Defence Industry Forum opening the proceedings at 10:00 a.m. local time, where a series of defence cooperation agreements are expected to be announced, Kyiv Post reported citing European Pravda.

The summit takes place amid a potential shift in Turkey's F-35 status. CNBC reports that President Trump hinted at making news during his visit 'related to jet engines and the potential sale of F-35 fighter jets' to Turkey. The State Department has already notified key lawmakers of plans to bypass congressional opposition to sell more than $700 million in F-110 jet engines to Ankara, which would power Turkey's domestically produced KAAN fighter. Turkey was expelled from the F-35 programme in 2019 after purchasing Russian S-400 missile systems. There remains significant bipartisan opposition on Capitol Hill to any F-35 sale while Turkey retains the S-400s.

On burden-sharing, NATO's supreme commander US Gen. Alex Grynkewich told AP that European allies have 'largely filled the gaps left by US reductions' to the NATO Force Model, after Washington announced on June 3 it would scale back contributions including a carrier group, aerial refuelling aircraft, and fighter jets. Former EU Commissioner Paolo Gentiloni said European states must use the summit to set conditions for the alliance's future while requiring the US to reaffirm its Article 5 commitment. Italy is reportedly seeking to soften language on a draft €70 billion military aid package for Ukraine, pushing to remove references to 2027.

Turkish authorities have arrested over 200 individuals ahead of the summit, and media accreditation has excluded opposition outlets — measures that former Ambassador Namik Tan criticised as unprecedented in NATO history.
sections.whyItMatters: The Ankara summit will test whether NATO can maintain cohesion as Trump pushes European allies to spend more while scaling back US commitments. A potential F-35 sale to Turkey would be a major strategic signal — rewarding Erdogan's loyalty while undermining the CAATSA principle that purchasing Russian arms carries consequences. The €70 billion Ukraine aid package and European force-generation pledges are the concrete outcomes to watch.

sections.heading: UK's £298 Billion Defence Investment Plan: Drones, GCAP, and a Post-Starmer Pivot
sections.body:
Outgoing UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer unveiled the £298 billion Defence Investment Plan covering the next four years, with £15 billion in additional spending beyond previous commitments. The plan focuses heavily on drone warfare: £5 billion is earmarked for drone transformation, including £650 million for expendable autonomous systems covering drones and uncrewed ground vehicles for the Army, Commando Force, and Special Forces, according to Firstpost and ADS Advance.

More than £8 billion is allocated to the Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP) over four years, building the next-generation stealth fighter alongside Japan and Italy — a programme that now gains additional significance following Germany's exit from the rival FCAS programme. The plan also includes a £50 billion defence export facility through UK Export Finance to support British defence firms winning contracts globally, and £115 million for AI threat defences including biosecurity and autonomous AI agent risks.

Defence Secretary Dan Jarvis said the plan 'sends a clear signal to our allies and our adversaries alike: Britain is stepping up on security.' The investment is projected to create around 60,000 extra direct and indirect UK industry jobs by the end of the decade. UK defence spending is 'on track' toward 2.7% of GDP, against Trump's demand for NATO members to reach 5% by 2035.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK plan is one of the largest single defence investment announcements by a European ally in recent years and directly addresses the drone and autonomous systems gap exposed by the Ukraine war. The £8 billion GCAP commitment also positions the UK-Italy-Japan programme as the clear European sixth-gen fighter path — now that FCAS has collapsed, GCAP may attract Germany as a new partner.

sections.heading: Germany's FCAS Exit and 'Team Gen 6' Reshape European Air Power
sections.body:
Germany has formally withdrawn from the manned fighter component of the Franco-German Future Combat Air System, ending Europe's most ambitious defence cooperation programme. Chancellor Friedrich Merz notified French President Macron on June 6, and the announcement came at the Berlin Air Show on June 8. The €100 billion programme, one-third German-funded, now survives only as a 'combat cloud' architecture and collaborative drone system, Aircraft Insider and The Aviationist reported.

At ILA Berlin 2026, eight German defence companies — Airbus, Autoflug, Diehl Defence, Hensoldt, Liebherr, MBDA Deutschland, MTU Aero Engines, and Rohde & Schwarz — signed a strategic positioning paper to form 'Team Gen 6' as a sovereign German sixth-generation fighter path. A parallel Spanish group including Indra, Airbus, Grupo Oesia, GMV, ITP Aero, and Sener is also forming. Airbus released a new concept aircraft with a gull-wing, canard design and single ventral DSI inlet — notably different from earlier FCAS renderings and drawing comparisons to Boeing's F-47. The concept is shown operating with the U760 Ravenstorm unmanned combat air vehicle and the FCAS Loyal Wingman drone.

Airbus Defence head Jean-Brice Dumont said industry is 'preparing options for the governments and the militaries' and stressed the need to 'safeguard areas where it works.' German Defence Minister Boris Pistorius called Team Gen 6 'conceivable and one possibility' but said alternatives are being considered, including acquiring additional F-35s or joining the UK-Italy-Japan GCAP/Tempest programme, which expects a demonstrator by 2027 and service entry by 2035.

The collapse was rooted in irreconcilable industrial differences. Dassault Aviation demanded up to 80% of the Next Generation Fighter workshare under its 'best athlete' model, while Airbus insisted on equal partnership. A formal mediation process launched after a Macron-Merz dinner on March 18 collapsed on April 18. French Defence Minister Catherine Vautrin told Europe 1 that 'Germany currently did not have the capacity to build a fighter jet.'
sections.whyItMatters: Europe now has two competing sixth-gen fighter paths — GCAP (UK-Italy-Japan) and Team Gen 6 (Germany-Spain) — where previously it had one collapsed programme. The fragmentation mirrors the 1980s Eurofighter split and will likely increase per-unit costs for all European nations. Germany's decision also creates an opening for GCAP to expand: Italy's Defence Minister explicitly invited Germany to join in December 2025, and the UK's £8 billion GCAP commitment gives the programme momentum.

sections.heading: US War-Ready Stockpile in Australia, Europe Fills NATO Gaps
sections.body:
The US Marine Corps is planning a permanent war-ready weapons stockpile on Australia's southeast coast, positioned beyond the range of most Chinese missiles. US Navy tender documents show $30 million allocated for warehouses and offices in Victoria state, with full capacity expected by 2028. The stockpile will be managed by a global defence contractor employing around 110 engineers, mechanics, and specialists, Channel News Asia reported, and includes crew-served weapons.

A Lowy Institute report warned that China can strike northern Australia with ballistic missiles from South China Sea outposts, making the southeastern placement a 'relevant consideration,' said Lowy's Sam Roggeveen. He also noted the facilities would be 'obvious targets for China' once operational. ANU professor John Blaxland said Australia's location gains significance given concerns about the vulnerability of Guam. The Pentagon has requested $500 million from Congress for prepositioning improvements across the Indo-Pacific.

In Europe, NATO's supreme commander Gen. Alex Grynkewich confirmed that European allies have 'largely filled the gaps left by US reductions' to the NATO Force Model after Washington announced it would scale back carrier, tanker, and fighter jet contributions to prioritise the Indo-Pacific. The UK elevated the emergency readiness status of F-35 aircraft and a second carrier. Grynkewich said that in areas where European nations lack matching capabilities, NATO is 'looking at alternate capabilities with matching effect,' Outlook India reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The Australian stockpile and European force generation are two faces of the same strategic shift: the US is distributing prepositioned equipment toward the Indo-Pacific while European allies scramble to cover the resulting gaps. The Marine Corps stockpile is the first of its kind in Australia and signals a long-term commitment to rotational force posture rather than permanent bases. In Europe, the question is whether the 'alternate capabilities' NATO is developing can credibly substitute for US air and naval power.

bottomLine: A convergence of procurement decisions, industrial realignments, and alliance politics is reshaping the defence landscape this week. India's ₹52,000 crore package makes counter-drone and air defence its top acquisition priority. The UK committed £298 billion with heavy drone and GCAP spending. Germany exited FCAS and formed Team Gen 6, fragmenting European fighter development into two competing paths. The F-35 fleet's 25% full mission capable rate casts a shadow over all these programmes — including a potential F-35 sale to Turkey at the Ankara summit. Meanwhile, the US is prepositioning war-ready stocks in Australia while European allies plug the gaps left by American redeployments, a dual-theatre balancing act that the NATO summit will stress-test starting July 7.
sources: Rediff, RNA Media, Weekly Blitz, Military Times, GAO, Air & Space Forces Magazine, CNBC, Kyiv Post, European Pravda, Outlook India, Channel News Asia, Lowy Institute, Firstpost, ADS Advance, The Aviationist, Aircraft Insider, Flight Global, Business Insider, Defense News, AP
